layer.label: Sinai Arabic NF M 8, Undertext (Undetermined (Roman))

note: Half-uncial with uncial G and long, straight ascenders and descenders. It would appear to be by a scribe trained in the West (perhaps insular), but they could be working in the Levant. Note the curious straight backed d with slightly detached round bow, as if the Latin letter-form has not been fully understood during copying (a feature that occurs in other early Latin manuscripts at St Catherine’s and which may suggest that scribes unfamiliar with details of western scripts were copying them) (Brown)

layer.label: Sinai Arabic NF M 8, Undertext (Undetermined (Arabic), Coptic)

note.value: Deed for the sale of a house by Aḥmad ibn 'Amr ibn Sulaymān (Muslim), who sold it to Tadrus ibn Ghalāṭ (Christian monk). The house seems to have been located in Ramla, Palestine (fol. 31v) (Treiger). The date (227 AH), which is hard to decipher, is found on folios 39r and 35r. On fol. fol. 30v, the date is given in Coptic letters
Description by Hikmat Kashouh, Alexander Treiger

layer.label: Sinai Arabic NF M 8, Undertext (Undetermined (Roman))

note: Small cursive Insular pointed minuscule which resembles Wearmouth/Jarrow Phase II minuscule of the compact rounded / tending to pointed variety introduced in the Moore Bede in the 740s-750s. There is a dedication cross at the top left of fol. 25, a feature found in Insular scriptural manuscripts from Wearmouth-Jarrow, and their derivatives, in the 8th century. The display capitals with multi-coloured infills and backgrounds (including a slate blue typical of the Mercian Tiberius group), however, resemble Mercian Tiberius group work of c. 800 (seen in Bodleian Hatton 93). The initial E formed of a Persian senmurv (a beast incorporating animal, bird, and fish features) on fol. 15v is of oriental style, with a ‘puzzle’ body recalling Frankish work but with a 'brontosaurus beast' Mercian Tiberius group animal head, reminiscent of those found in the Barberini Gospels and the Book of Cerne around 800-830. A senmurv also appears as a zoomorphic initial in the Mercian Royal Prayerbook (BL, Royal 2.A.xx) of c. 800. The style of script recalls Mercian Tiberius group mss of c. 800 also in the pointed aspect. This mixture of influences is typical of Latin manuscripts from Sinai which were probably made there. This is probably part of the same Insular Gospel book as that found on fols. 15, 19, 25r, 26 and 27 (Brown)

The remaining fragments indicate that this was a bifolium from a small format Gospel book of Insular pocket type. Its layout is somewhat squat, inclining to square rather than rectangular page layout, which is unusual in western manuscripts (but not eastern) (Brown)

note.value: Identification of script and text by Michelle Brown and Daniela Mairhofer
The text is that of Jerome’s Vulgate edition; there are a couple of minor variants, but from the small sample of text that remains it seems to be a good Vulgate, perhaps of Northumbrian type (Brown).
Description by Michelle P. Brown, David Ganz, Daniela Mairhofer

layer.label: Sinai Arabic NF M 8, Undertext (Undetermined (Roman))

note: The scale of the text is large and, by analogy with other de luxe late antique illustrated works (such as the Vatican Virgil), it is likely to have been laid out in single columns with images (outline drawings) arranged as column pictures and some perhaps as full-page miniatures. In the illustration on fol. 18r, only the heads of what would probably have been full-length standing figures remain, indicating the very large scale of the original page. The text in the Ottonian copy from Werden (now in Budapest) has two columns, but in smaller Carolingian minuscule script and was probably reconfigured

note.value: Text identification by David Ganz. Further details provided by Michelle Brown, who also wrote the following comments. Lower layer of double palimpsest. The History of Apollonius of Tyre (Historia Apollonii regis Tyri) is an anonymous 5th- or 6th-century CE Latin (illustrated) version of a Greek original text. The Latin version is first mentioned by Venantius Fortunatus in the late 6th century (Carmina, Book vi.8, 11.5-6). The copy in this ms. predates the other known (9th-10th century) Latin versions of the text by several centuries. It is of very fine quality, and probably hails from Rome. It provides evidence of the sort of late antique illustrated cycle of this novel in the earliest medieval illustrated copy, an Ottonian fragment in Hungary (National Széchényi Library, COD. Lat. 4), made in Werden c1000 and containing some 38 line drawings in crude style, but probably ultimately based on something similar to the Sinai fragment. The surviving fragmentary image (fol. 18r) depicts a young woman and a man, set beneath an architectural entablature with Corinthian column capitals. The subject is likely Apollonius and his daughter, Tarsia, at the house of Strangulos (probably introducing ch. 28, the middle section of which is on fol. 18r). Both script and text are confident and expert. The line-drawing style is loose but adept. The illustration was palimpsested and overwritten in a 7th-8th-century half-uncial hand of western or local Sinaite Latin origin, written upside down in relation to the illustration. Andras Nemeth has identified the text as the Athanasian Creed, a text that became crucial to East-West church relations from the 9th century onwards, due to the western espousal of the controversial ‘filioque’ clause. (Brown).
Description by Michelle P. Brown, David Ganz, Daniela Mairhofer

This text was written by the same scribe who copied the UTO Basil of Caesarea, Homily on the Martyr Julitta in this codex. These two texts belonged to the same original manuscript

Fragment 21r (upper outer piece), 21v (upper outer piece) possibly also belongs to this text; fragments 86r (gutter lower piece), 86v (gutter lower piece), 116r (lower piece), 116v (lower piece), could also belong to the UTO Basil of Caesarea, "On the Martyr Julitta" (Rossetto)

Bibliography: Boudalis and Rossetto ; Rossetto 2023b
